title: The PMM's Hippocratic Oath
subtitle: Responsible GenAI in an era of infinite persuasion

slides.title: The PMM's Hippocratic Oath
slides.subtitle: Responsible GenAI in an era of infinite persuasion.
bullets: Promise → Peril → Path

slides.title: Persuasion scaled. Verification didn't.
slides.lead: We hired an intern who can write 10,000 ads a minute… and cannot be deposed.
bullets: GenAI turns messaging into an always-on persuasion machine — volume, personalization, experimentation, all at once.
The question isn't whether we use it. The question is whether we've built the check engine light.
You leave with a repeatable pre-publish scaffolding you can pilot on Monday. Not next quarter. Monday.

slides.title: The AI flywheel is real
slides.lead: We went from 'launch in a quarter' to 'launch in a browser tab.' That's genuine leverage.
bullets: Battlecards: 12 variants drafted in 2 hours. Previously: 2 weeks.
Launch messaging: A/B tested 8 positioning angles in one sprint. Previously: 2.
Enablement: Turned a 40-page product spec into 6 customer-facing one-pagers overnight.
Localization: Adapted messaging for 4 regions in a day, not a quarter.

slides.title: The failure modes of infinite persuasion
slides.lead: These aren't edge cases. They're default bugs. The system ships them unless you design them out.

matrix.q1: Liability Laundering: 'The chatbot said it' doesn't reduce liability. Your brand published it.
matrix.q2: Hallucinated Authority: AI drafts 'evidence posture' faster than review can verify.
matrix.q3: Authenticity Breaks: Voice consistency is easy. Values consistency is hard.
matrix.q4: Erasure: AI defaults to the majority persona. Edge-case customers get optimized away.

slides.title: Receipt 1: Deloitte
slides.lead: The citations were… aspirational.
footnotes: Deloitte partially refunded an Australian government contract after a report contained erroneous references.
bullets: AI drafted footnotes, references, the whole academic aesthetic — faster than anyone could verify.
The output looked like evidence. It shipped like evidence. Until they checked the links.
Lesson: if you show citations, maintain them. They're a product feature, not decoration.

slides.title: Receipt 2: Air Canada
slides.lead: A chatbot is a salesperson who never sleeps… until it freelances your refund policy.
footnotes: A tribunal found Air Canada liable for inaccurate chatbot advice about bereavement fares.
bullets: Chatbot made a promise the airline's actual policy didn't support.
Air Canada's defense: 'The chatbot is a separate legal entity.' Tribunal: No.
Lesson: if your AI can make a promise — about pricing, capability, or policy — define who signs off.

slides.title: Receipt 3: Coca-Cola
slides.lead: The internet's review: 'This ad has the emotional range of a spreadsheet.'
footnotes: Coca-Cola's AI holiday ads drew backlash as 'soulless' — even as the company highlighted production speed.
bullets: The output sounded like Coca-Cola. Had the colors, the typography, the jingle.
But people could feel the gap between voice match and values match.
You can nail every item in the style guide and still lose the customer. Ask Coke's CMO.

slides.title: Not bans. Held tensions.
slides.lead: Policies are like gym memberships. Buying one isn't the workout.
bullets: Bans don't scale. People route around them.
Doctors have the Hippocratic Oath. It doesn't say 'don't use scalpels.' It says 'first, do no harm.'
We need the same thing for product marketing.

slides.title: The oath
slides.lead: When persuasion scales infinitely, I will protect trust.
slides.roof: When persuasion scales infinitely, I will protect trust.
slides.foundation: A PMM Code of Conduct — not rules, a practice.

pillars.title: Verification
items: I will not publish what I have not checked.
I will enhance relevance, not exploit vulnerability.

pillars.title: Stewardship
items: Tokens are not free — not to my company, not to the planet.

pillars.title: Integrity
items: I will not let AI speak words my brand would not stand behind.
I will notice whose pain points get erased.

slides.title: Tension 1: Speed vs verification
slides.lead: I will not publish what I have not checked.

matrix.q1: The Goal: Rapid, auditable generation via strict templates
matrix.q2: The Trap: Slow manual writing disguised as 'safety'
matrix.q3: Legacy Process: Slow manual writing with poor QA
matrix.q4: The Danger Zone: Raw ChatGPT output pasted into market

left.label: Ships without a Claim Card
left.body: Our platform will reduce your incident response time by 40%.
details: No source. No scope. No conditions.
Sales sends it → prospect quotes it in a contract
POC disproves it → trust lost before onboarding starts

right.label: Ships with a Claim Card
right.body: Teams using [feature] can reduce mean incident response time by up to 40% in [environment], based on [study].
details: Source: linked. Scope: bounded.
Sales can quote it. Legal can defend it.
Pro tip: rewrite 'will' as 'can' + conditions.

slides.title: Claim Card — let GenAI draft it for you
slides.lead: A claim without evidence is just vibes with punctuation.
bullets: Prompt: 'Review [this asset]. Extract every claim. For each, fill a Claim Card: source, scope, reproducibility, harm tier. Flag anything unverifiable.'
Attach output to battlecards, datasheets, launch narratives, analyst briefs.
If the asset makes a claim that could be quoted in a contract — it needs a card. Let AI find them; humans verify them.

slides.title: Example Claim Card
slides.lead: Every high-impact claim must be attached to a verifiable, reproducible card before publication.

card.claim: Our platform reduces customer churn by 34% within the first 90 days of deployment.
card.verdict: ⚠ Requires Legal Review Before External Use

slides.title: Tension 2: Personalization vs manipulation
slides.lead: I will enhance relevance, not exploit vulnerability.
bullets: If you wouldn't say the targeting logic out loud to the customer, don't put it in a prompt.
Vulnerability zones: health, finance, grief, addiction, minors, time pressure + fear language
Benefit Alignment Test: Who benefits? What vulnerability? Would you disclose? What would a regulator call this?

left.label: Optimized for the company
left.body: Write 20 urgency-driven nurture emails for users who visited pricing 3+ times. Emphasize that prices increase Friday. Reference competitor switching data.
details: 'Prices increase Friday' — true or manufactured urgency?
'Teams like yours are switching' — social proof or a pressure tactic?
Optimizing for conversion anxiety, not customer clarity

right.label: Optimized for the customer
right.body: Write 5 helpful variants for pricing-page visitors. Focus on clarifying the pricing model, linking the ROI calculator, and offering a 15-min walkthrough.
details: Same audience. Same goal — convert.
But the prompt answers the question the prospect actually has.
Offers a conversation, not a countdown timer.

slides.title: Tension 3: Efficiency vs stewardship
slides.lead: Tokens are like Pringles. The can says 'share size.' It is lying.
slides.source: IEA Energy & AI Report + Pew Research, 2024–25
bullets: Simpler approach first — escalate as complexity increases
Three modes: Fast (chat, templates) → Think Deeper (reasoning, multi-step) → Deep Research (synthesis, multi-source)
U.S. data center electricity could more than double by 2030 (IEA). Every unnecessary escalation contributes.

slides.title: Efficiency vs stewardship — before / after
footnotes: IEA: U.S. data centers ~183 TWh (2024), projected ~426 TWh (2030)

left.label: Always Deep Research mode
left.body: 15 calls to a frontier model. 3 usable emails. ~$4.50. No human reviewed the final versions.
details: 'Be creative, surprise me' — every single time
Full system context on every call. 20% hit rate.
Like using a helicopter to go to the corner store.

right.label: Right mode for the job
right.body: Fast: 1 golden template. Think Deeper: adapt for 12 regions. Total: ~$0.40. Human reviewed diffs.
details: Human writes the positioning (Fast). Model adapts it (Think Deeper).
Deep Research only when synthesizing multiple conflicting sources.
10x cheaper. 4x more output. And someone actually read it.

slides.title: Tension 4: 'Sounds like us' vs 'is us'
slides.lead: I will not let AI speak words my brand would not stand behind.
slides.preamble: Brand Signature Test — one question, thirty seconds:
slides.preambleBody: If this sentence is quoted in a lawsuit, a regulator letter, or a front-page story — do we stand by it without backpedaling?

bullets.title: Press
bullets.description: Does it hold up to scrutiny?

slides.title: 'Sounds like us' vs 'is us' — before / after

left.label: Fails the Brand Signature Test
left.body: Our AI-powered security engine autonomously detects and eliminates threats in real time — so your team never has to worry about breaches again.
details: Lawsuit? Indefensible.
Regulator? 'Autonomously' is a promise.
Front page? 'Never worry' is a dare to every pen tester alive.

right.label: Passes the Brand Signature Test
right.body: Our AI-assisted detection surfaces potential threats faster — so your security team can respond with better context.
details: Lawsuit? Bounded. Sourced.
Regulator? 'Assisted,' not 'autonomous.'
'In testing, mean detection time improved by [X]% under [conditions].' Your GC can live with this.

slides.title: Tension 5a: Who converts vs who gets erased
slides.lead: I will notice whose pain points AI amplifies — and whose get erased.
bullets: AI defaults to the majority persona. Non-technical teams, regulated industries, accessibility — they don't show up.
Coverage review: tell the model who to include, and ask it to flag where the product falls short.
When you optimize for aggregate conversion, you smooth away the customers who don't fit the default. That's not optimization. That's deletion.

slides.title: Who converts vs who gets erased — before / after

left.label: Default-only persona coverage
left.body: Generate customer stories for our project management tool.
details: AI output: 3 stories. All 50-person eng teams, US-based, English-speaking.
Missing: non-technical teams, regulated industries, accessibility, small orgs.
It's not malicious — it's statistical. But the result is the same.

right.label: Coverage-reviewed
right.body: Generate customer stories. Ensure coverage: a 5-person non-profit ops team, a regulated healthcare workflow, a team using assistive technology.
details: Ask the model to flag where the product falls short for each persona.
That's coverage, not cosplay.
Inclusive by design. Honest about edges.

slides.title: Tension 5b: Style laundering
slides.lead: If you borrow a living person's commercial craft: license, attribute, or translate into explicit constraints.
slides.source: OpenAI Sora System Card + US Copyright Office + WIPO + Waits v. Frito-Lay
bullets: 'Write in the style of [living writer]' is plagiarism with extra steps.
Style Card: Tone, structure, devices, constraints. No names. No laundering.
The legal floor is not the ethical ceiling. Reputational risk is entirely predictable.

slides.title: Style laundering — before / after
slides.source: Waits v. Frito-Lay | OpenAI Sora System Card

left.label: Named imitation
left.body: Write this launch narrative in the style of Simon Sinek.
details: Commercial value extracted from a living person's craft.
No credit. No consent. No license.
One tweet from the creator and you've lost the argument before Legal picks up the phone.

right.label: Style Card — explicit constraints
right.body: Tone: earnest, plainspoken, slightly provocative. Structure: start with purpose, expand to evidence, close with action. Devices: rhetorical questions, short parallelism, contrast pairs.
details: Same creative output. No ethical debt.
Constraints are yours. The voice is yours.
If an approach heavily shaped the work, disclose influences — the way research cites sources.

slides.title: Post-GenAI decision architecture
slides.lead: Ethics without architecture is personal heroics. Personal heroics don't survive headcount changes.

slides.title: What you leave with
slides.lead: Yes, we're marketers. We don't do vows. Except brand promises — which are vows with better typography.
bullets: Five held tensions — not rules. A practice you run every time you ship.
A toolkit — Claim Card, Benefit Alignment Test, Brand Signature Test, Coverage Review, Style Card. All promptable.
A decision architecture — GenAI-augmented pre-publish checklist with multi-model consensus and self-learning feedback loops.

slides.title: Close + Q&A
slides.subtitle: Where does this break in your org?
bullets: What is your highest-risk claim in market right now?
Where does verification fail in your workflow?
Which tension is hardest in your org?
What can you pilot by next week?

bullets.title: HBR now asks
bullets.description: 'Did you use Generative AI tools to aid in your submission? If yes, how?' — Should your team?

slides.title: Toolkit: Claim Card
slides.lead: Prompt: 'Review [this asset]. Extract every claim. For each, fill a Claim Card: source, scope, repro, harm tier. Flag anything unverifiable.'

slides.title: Toolkit: Benefit Alignment Test
slides.lead: Prompt: 'Evaluate [this campaign brief]. For each targeting rule, run the Benefit Alignment Test. Flag any vulnerability targeting.'

card.claim: Benefit Alignment Test — Personalization Ethics Gate

fields.label: Vulnerability Targeted
fields.value: None | Health | Finance | Grief | Addiction | Minors | Time Pressure

fields.label: Disclose Targeting?
fields.value: Would you explain the logic to the person being targeted?

card.verdict: If any answer is 'No' or 'Unsure' → Revise before shipping

slides.title: Toolkit: Brand Signature Test
slides.lead: Prompt: 'Take [this asset]. For every sentence making a promise, run the Brand Signature Test against all four audiences. Rewrite anything that fails.'

card.claim: Brand Signature Test — Would you stand by this line?

fields.label: On the Front Page?
fields.value: Does it hold up to scrutiny?

card.verdict: If any answer is No → Rewrite with bounded scope + conditions

slides.title: Starter prompts — use GenAI for all of this
slides.lead: The whole point is that these tools are promptable. Copy, paste, adapt.

bullets.title: Claim Extraction
bullets.description: Review [asset]. List every factual claim. For each: source URL, scope, reproducibility, harm tier.

bullets.title: Benefit Alignment
bullets.description: Audit [campaign]. Per segment: who benefits? Vulnerability targeted? Would we disclose the logic to the recipient?

bullets.title: Brand Signature
bullets.description: Read [asset] as four people: plaintiff's attorney, FTC reviewer, journalist, customer. Flag lines that change across readings.

bullets.title: Coverage Review
bullets.description: Generate 5 customer stories for [product]. Audit: which personas are missing? Where does it fall short for non-default users?

bullets.title: Style Card
bullets.description: Decompose [doc]'s writing style into: tone, structure, rhetorical devices, constraints. No named persons. Output a reusable Style Card.
